images.caption: Figure 1:Distribution Contractive RL (DICE-RL)refines (a) a pretrained generative BC policy (i.e., the behavior prior) into (b) a “pro” policy by contracting the action distribution around successful action modes. DICE-RL leverages the generative behavior cloning policy to achieve (c) controllable exploration for sample-efficient and stable reinforcement learning.

Figure 2:Comparisons on Robomimic.Success rate versus online environment steps for Robomimic tasks under RL finetuning. Top row: state observations; bottom row: pixel observations. Curves are averaged over 5 random seeds, with evaluation on 300 held-out test configurations; shaded regions denote variability across seeds.
Figure 3:DICE-RL using either Proficient-Human (PH) or Multi-Human (MH) data. Top: success-rate curves. Bottom: finetunability metrics (GoodCov/BadCov/BadEnt).
Figure 4:DICE-RL finetuning with pretrained BC checkpoints trained for different numbers of epochs. Left: success-rate curves. Right: finetunability metrics (GoodCov/BadCov/BadEnt).

Figure 5:Value improvement (Δ​V\Delta V) vs action entropy reduction (Δ​H\Delta H). Larger gains in value are accompanied by larger drops in action entropy, indicating that RL sharpens the pretrained action distribution to high-value actions.

Figure 6:A representative rollout trajectory of the RL policy onTool Hang, together with the running change in action entropy and value improvement. We zoom in on frames where value improvement spikes and action entropy drops; these states are often critical for task success (e.g., pre-insertion and insertion). In contrast, during free-space motions that are less consequential for success, we observe less reduction in action entropy

Figure 7:(Left) Pairwise distance over time between two rollouts from different initial states,|st−st′|22|s0−s0′|22\frac{|s_{t}-s^{\prime}_{t}|_{2}^{2}}{|s_{0}-s^{\prime}_{0}|_{2}^{2}}, comparing our RL policy to expert demonstrations. The rollouts from RL policy exhibit more stable distance evolution, indicating stronger contraction. (Right) Success rate under action noise injection, where the probability of perturbing the action output increases from 0.1 to 0.9. Our RL policy degrades more gracefully than an expert BC policy with a similar initial success rate.

Figure 8:Real robot experiment.(a)The dominant failure modes of the pretrained BC policies for all three tasks.(b)After RL finetuning, DICE-RL works reliably on all tasks.(c)Success rate versus number of online episodes.

Figure 9:A representativeBeltAssemblyrollout with running change in action entropy (Δ​H\Delta H) and value improvement (Δ​V\Delta V), with the largest entropy drop and value gains occurring around critical contact transitions.
Figure 10:The BC-loss filter prevents RL finetuning from being bottlenecked by the pretrained policy.
Figure 11:Multi-sample expectation training improves RL finetuning sample efficiency, with largerKKyielding faster gains.
Figure 12:Best-of-NNaction selection during online interaction accelerates RL finetuning convergence to peak performance. Dashed lines indicate performance of pretrained BC policies.
Figure 13:DICE-RL achieves stable, sample-efficient RL finetuning for both pretrained flow policies and diffusion policies.
Figure 14:DICE-RL finetuning with varying number of demonstrations used for BC policy pretraining.
Figure 15:DICE-RL finetuning with varying number of gradient steps per RL update.
Figure 16:DICE-RL finetuning with varying number of N-step return.
Figure 17:DICE-RL finetuning with varying number of critic networks used in the critic ensemble.
Figure 18:DICE-RL finetuning with varying BC loss coefficients.
Figure 19:DICE-RL finetuning with varying RLPD schedule.
